{{Short description|Neutral elementary particle with extremely small mass}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Neutrino
| image = First neutrino observation.jpg
| caption = First observation of a neutrino interaction in a [[bubble chamber]] (1970).
| topics = [[Weak interaction]], [[Beta decay]], [[Lepton]], [[Particle physics]]
| composition = [[Elementary particle]]
| statistics = [[Fermion]]
| symbol = ν
| types = νₑ, ν_μ, ν_τ (electron, muon, tau)
| mass = < 0.8 eV (electron neutrino)
| charge = 0
| spin = 1/2
}}

A '''neutrino''' (denoted by the Greek letter ν) is a [[fermion]] (an [[elementary particle]] with half-integer [[spin (physics)|spin]]) that interacts only via the [[weak interaction]] and [[gravity]]. The neutrino has a very small [[rest mass]] and no [[electric charge]]. It is one of the most abundant particles in the [[universe]], yet it rarely interacts with matter — trillions pass through your body every second without any effect.

== History ==
The neutrino was first postulated by [[Wolfgang Pauli]] in 1930 to explain the continuous energy spectrum of [[beta decay]] while preserving conservation of [[energy]], [[momentum]], and [[angular momentum]]. It was named by [[Enrico Fermi]] and first experimentally detected in 1956 by [[Clyde Cowan]] and [[Frederick Reines]].

== Properties ==

=== Weak interaction ===
Neutrinos interact only through the [[weak interaction]] (and gravity), making them extremely difficult to detect. The [[cross section (physics)|cross section]] for neutrino–nucleon interaction is roughly 10⁻⁴³ cm², meaning a neutrino would need to travel through about one [[light-year]] of lead to have a 50% chance of interacting.

=== Neutrino oscillation ===
{{Main|Neutrino oscillation}}
Neutrinos exist in three "flavours" (electron, muon, tau) that can transform into one another as they travel — a phenomenon called [[neutrino oscillation]]. This discovery proved that neutrinos have non-zero mass, a result awarded the [[Nobel Prize in Physics]] in 2015.

=== Beta decay ===
{{Main|Beta decay}}
In [[beta decay]], a [[neutron]] transforms into a [[proton]], emitting an [[electron]] and an electron antineutrino:
:<math>n \rightarrow p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e</math>

== Sources ==
* [[Sun]] — solar neutrinos from [[nuclear fusion]]
* [[Cosmic ray]] interactions in the atmosphere
* [[Supernova]]e — 99% of the gravitational binding energy is released as neutrinos
* [[Nuclear reactor]]s — beta decay of fission products
* [[Particle accelerator]]s — neutrino beams
* [[Big Bang]] — cosmic neutrino background

== Detection ==
Major neutrino observatories:
* [[Super-Kamiokande]] (Japan) — water Cherenkov detector
* [[IceCube Neutrino Observatory]] (Antarctica) — cubic-kilometre detector in ice
* [[Sudbury Neutrino Observatory]] (Canada) — heavy water detector

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Beta decay]]
* [[Lepton]]
* [[Neutrino oscillation]]
* [[Particle physics]]
* [[Weak interaction]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Neutrinos| ]]
[[Category:Leptons]]
[[Category:Weak interaction]]
[[Category:Elementary particles]]
